I had the honor of serving as a community volunteer and leader with the Islamic Society of Greater Lafayette from January 2023 through December 2024, where I played a central role in guiding and coordinating a large and diverse group of students and community members. In this role, I worked closely with individuals across a wide range of ages, cultural backgrounds, and lived experiences, helping to foster an environment rooted in inclusion, mutual respect, and long-term sustainability. My responsibilities extended beyond logistics or coordination; I was deeply involved in shaping the direction of community initiatives to ensure that they were thoughtful, strategic, and responsive to the needs of those they were meant to serve. This experience required constant reflection, adaptability, and a willingness to listen carefully, particularly when navigating differing priorities or perspectives within the community.

Alongside this leadership work, I took on an informal but meaningful mentoring role within the community, offering guidance and support to individuals navigating both personal and collective challenges. I frequently worked with community members who were learning how to step into leadership themselves, helping them develop confidence, communicate effectively across differences, and resolve conflicts in constructive ways. Through these relationships, I gained firsthand experience in balancing empathy with accountability, and in creating space for others to contribute meaningfully without imposing a single vision or approach. This aspect of the role reinforced my belief that sustainable leadership is less about authority and more about empowering others to grow into their own sense of responsibility and agency.

One of the most formative lessons from this experience came through a long-term effort to increase women’s participation in mosque activities and community life. Although women made up a substantial portion of the broader Muslim community, cultural expectations and structural barriers had historically limited their visibility and involvement. Over the course of two years, I contributed to initiatives aimed at addressing these challenges by creating more welcoming spaces and encouraging dialogue around participation and inclusion. Progress was often slow and, at times, discouraging, as early efforts did not yield immediate or visible results. However, by remaining committed to the work and allowing time for trust and cultural shifts to develop organically, the community eventually saw a meaningful increase in female engagement. This experience taught me the importance of patience, resilience, and long-term thinking when working toward systemic or cultural change, as well as the value of trusting the process even when outcomes are not immediately apparent.

Earlier in my academic journey, I volunteered with Purdue University’s Earth, Atmospheric, and Planetary Sciences department as part of an educational outreach effort focused on younger audiences. During this time, I helped design and deliver interactive programming for elementary school students, particularly in the context of Earth Day. I worked to translate complex scientific concepts into accessible and engaging ideas, including using the familiar notion of barcodes to explain how scientists detect and analyze the atmospheres of exoplanets. I also led discussions and activities centered on energy conservation and reducing household carbon footprints, with the goal of empowering children to understand how small, everyday actions can contribute to broader environmental responsibility. This experience strengthened my ability to communicate technical ideas clearly and creatively, while remaining attentive to the curiosity and perspectives of non-technical audiences.

I later had the opportunity to serve as a guest speaker at West Lafayette Junior/Senior High School, where I presented to middle and high school students on the role of mathematics in both theoretical and applied contexts. Drawing from examples in atmospheric dynamics and operator theory, I demonstrated how mathematical thinking can simplify complex systems and reveal underlying structure in real-world phenomena. Beyond the technical content, I spoke openly about the nature of research, the nonlinear path of learning, and the personal growth that comes from grappling with difficult ideas over time. This experience reinforced my commitment to demystifying advanced subjects and encouraging students to see intellectual struggle as a meaningful and necessary part of growth.

Most recently, I contributed as a speaker and workshop facilitator with Climate Resilient Communities, where I worked on youth-focused programming centered on climate data storytelling and citizen science. In this role, I helped co-design workshops that combined discussion, interactive activities, and local data to make climate issues more tangible and relevant to participants’ lived experiences. A particular focus of this work was helping young people craft and communicate climate narratives that centered marginalized communities, using gamified approaches to encourage creativity and collaboration. I also introduced accessible data collection and interpretation methods designed for non-technical audiences, bridging the gap between technical tools and community-driven knowledge. Through this work, I aimed to equip participants with practical skills to engage their own communities in meaningful conversations about climate resilience and data-informed action.

Across these experiences, my community involvement has consistently shaped how I approach leadership, communication, and problem-solving. They have reinforced the importance of empathy, adaptability, and perseverance, and have taught me to value long-term impact over immediate results. These lessons continue to inform how I engage with complex systems, whether social, technical, or organizational, and remain central to how I envision my professional and civic contributions moving forward.

[Purdue Earth, Atmospheric, and Planetary Sciences](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karimme/details/volunteer-experiences/edit/forms/1028607703/)  
[Feb 2022 \- Apr 2022 · 3 mos](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karimme/details/volunteer-experiences/edit/forms/1028607703/)  
[Education](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karimme/details/volunteer-experiences/edit/forms/1028607703/)

\- Used the familiar concept of barcodes to explain to elementary school students how scientists detect and analyze the atmospheres of exoplanets, highlighting how both systems encode and reveal hidden information.

\- Taught children about energy conservation and reducing their carbon footprint at home in celebration of Earth Day. The session was designed to be engaging and understandable, inspiring them to take simple, meaningful actions.

# Extracurricular Activities

\- Served as Vice President and religious chair of the Muslim Student Association and as Secretary of the Meteorological Association at Purdue University.  
\- Mentored international students in the College of Science through Global Science Partners.  
\- Engaged in community outreach, including educational videos on exoplanets and climate change for Earth Day and leading elementary school camps.
